frame_analyses.description: First instrument enters the field and the trainee begins orienting the needle for grasping.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Entry is controlled, but there is a brief setup interval before the needle is efficiently presented.

frame_analyses.description: Needle is aligned near the target and readied for the tissue pass.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle angle becomes acceptable, though there are a few small regrips before committing to the bite.

frame_analyses.description: The needle traverses the Penrose and the stitch is brought through both target sides.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Placement appears close to the marked region with generally atraumatic handling.

frame_analyses.description: A broad initial loop pattern is created for the first knot throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The first throw appears consistent with a surgeon's knot, with the right instrument likely leading while the left serves as the post; exact visualization of both wraps is partially obscured.

frame_analyses.description: The first knot is seated down onto the Penrose after the initial looping sequence.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The knot settles without obvious slip, but seating is slower than ideal.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is formed with the opposite hand pattern and the strand is drawn through the loop.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: A hand switch appears to occur correctly here, with the left instrument likely taking the lead; extra regrasping prolongs the phase.

frame_analyses.description: The final throw is constructed and the knot stack is tightened down.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The right instrument appears to resume the lead for the third throw, completing an apparent right-left-right alternation.

frame_analyses.description: The knot is stable while the trainee repositions the tails and prepares to cut.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: This is the least efficient portion of the run, with prolonged tail management before the final cuts.

frame_analyses.description: The final tail is cut and the instruments withdraw, leaving the drain secured on the block.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: No avulsion or gross knot failure is visible at task end.

knot_assessments.notes: First throw has a broader double-wrap appearance consistent with a surgeon's knot; the right instrument appears to lead, though the exact second wrap is partially obscured.
Second throw appears to switch to a left-led pattern and is seated without obvious gross slip, but it requires multiple regrips.
Third throw appears to switch back to a right-led pattern and the final knot stack remains intact after tightening.

technique_summary: The trainee completed the task without avulsion or obvious knot failure, and the final approximation appears stable. Most time loss came from repeated regrasping during the second and third throws and prolonged tail management before cutting, while needle handling and tissue respect were otherwise controlled. Competent but inefficient: the trainee produced an acceptable final result and avoided major rubric violations, but the knot-tying sequence was slower and less economical than ideal.
improvement_suggestions: Reduce time between throws by deciding earlier which limb is the post and which is the working end before starting each half-hitch.
Seat the first double throw more compactly with fewer intermediate regrips so the knot stack lies flatter before progressing.
Introduce the cutter sooner and stabilize both tails against the knot before cutting to shorten the end phase.
strengths: Maintained control of the Penrose throughout the task with no drain avulsion.
Apparent hand alternation was preserved across the three throws (right-left-right).
Final knot remained intact after tightening, with no obvious gross unraveling at the end.

rubric_assessment.overall_judgment: Not a failure-of-construct performance — a time-loss performance. Trainee produced an acceptable final result and avoided major rubric violations, but the knot-tying sequence was slower and less economical than ideal. Coaching focus: faster, more deliberate throw construction and cleaner tail management.
biggest_time_losses: Second throw at 58s was the single biggest bottleneck (post/working-limb uncertainty)
Third throw at 30.2s — still slow despite simpler geometry
Prolonged tail management before final cuts
rubric_assessment.uncertainty_notes: Moderate confidence overall. Most confident about absence of avulsion/gap and overall timing. Less confident about exact mm deviation and the exact wrap count on the first throw.
